appetitizer: Small dishes designed to start a meal: quick to prepare, generous on flavour, light enough not to crowd what follows.
baking: Sweet and savoury baking foundations, covering pastries, custards, meringues and the techniques that underpin them.
baking/cremes: Pastry creams, crème pâtissière and crème anglaise, the silky bases of countless desserts.
baking/meringue: French, Italian and Swiss meringues for piping, baking and folding into desserts.
baking/pastry: Shortcrust, puff, choux and laminated doughs, the building blocks for tarts, pies and patisserie.
base-ingredients: The building blocks: spice mixes, marinades, pastes and prepared bases that sit behind every other recipe in the collection.
base-ingredients/baking: Sweet bases such as frangipane, sugar syrups and almond cream, used inside other bakes.
base-ingredients/curry-paste: Wet pastes from Thai, Indian and other traditions that form the backbone of curries.
base-ingredients/curry-powder: Dry blends ground for curries, dals and dry rubs.
base-ingredients/herbs: Herb blends, oils and pastes for finishing or flavouring.
base-ingredients/marinade: Wet and dry preparations for tenderising and seasoning protein before cooking.
base-ingredients/sambal: Indonesian-style chilli condiments: fiery, aromatic, served at the table or stirred into the pan.
base-ingredients/sauces: Foundational sauces used as components in larger recipes.
base-ingredients/spices: Whole and ground spice blends, including creole, jerk, garam masala and many more.
base-ingredients/syrup: Sweet syrups for soaking sponges, glazing pastries and finishing desserts.
base-ingredients/vegetables: Prepared vegetable bases, including purées, confit and aromatics.
bread-pasta: Yeasted breads, flatbreads, fresh pasta and noodles. Recipes that reward time and a steady hand.
bread-pasta/making-bread: Yeasted loaves, sourdough and flatbreads. Methods and master doughs.
coulis: Smooth, sieved purées for finishing plates and dressing desserts: fruit on the sweet side, vegetable on the savoury.
coulis/fruit: Sieved fruit purées such as raspberry, mango and blackberry, for plating desserts.
coulis/vegetable: Savoury vegetable purées for finishing meat and fish dishes.
cuisine: A region-by-region tour through global cooking traditions, from Cajun gumbos and Indian curries to Italian ragùs and Vietnamese pho. Each section captures its cuisine's signature flavours, key ingredients and the techniques that shape its character.
cuisine/cajun: Louisiana cooking born of French, Spanish, African and Caribbean influences. Heat and depth come from the holy trinity of onion, celery and bell pepper, layered with cayenne, paprika, bay and smoked sausage. Defining techniques are the long-cooked roux, blackening over high heat and one-pot rice dishes like jambalaya and gumbo.
cuisine/chinese: A cuisine of regional variety, spanning Cantonese restraint, Sichuan numbing heat, Hunan smoke and northern wheat tradition. Aromatics lean on garlic, ginger and spring onion, with depth from soy, rice wine, sesame oil and fermented bean pastes. Wok-based stir-frying, steaming, red-braising and dough work for dumplings and noodles dominate the kitchen.
cuisine/cuban: Cuba on a plate, where Spanish, African and Caribbean strands are woven together with rice, beans, pork and citrus. Sofrito (onion, garlic, pepper, cumin), oregano and bay set the base, while sour-orange mojo brightens roasted and braised meats. Long gentle braising sits alongside griddled and fried preparations.
cuisine/french: The grammar of Western cooking: refined regional and bistro traditions built on butter, cream, herbs and stocks. Shallot, mustard, wine and brandy do much of the seasoning, with mother sauces and reductions giving every dish its finish. Slow braises (boeuf bourguignon, coq au vin), pan sauces and patisserie technique anchor the canon.
cuisine/indian: A vast, regional cuisine where spice work is the defining art. Blends like garam masala, panch phoron and individual masala mixes layer warmth, heat and aromatics across curries, rice, breads and pickles. Tandoor cooking, slow-simmered gravies, dum-style steaming and the British curry-house BIR method (pre-cooked meat finished in seconds in spiced base gravy) all sit here.
cuisine/indian/Base: Sauces, purées and base gravies that underpin Indian curries.
cuisine/indian/Breads: Naans, rotis, parathas and pooris, leavened and unleavened.
cuisine/indian/Meals: Main-course curries: chicken, lamb and vegetable, across regional and curry-house styles.
cuisine/indian/Meals/BIR: British Indian Restaurant style: pre-cooked meat and a base gravy joined with spices in a fast finish, the curry-house technique.
cuisine/indian/pastes: Wet aromatic pastes such as onion, cashew and ginger-garlic, used as flavour bases for curries.
cuisine/indian/rice: Biryanis, pilafs and aromatic rice dishes.
cuisine/indian/sauces-pickles: Chutneys, raitas and pickles to serve alongside curries.
cuisine/indian/Spice-Mixes: Garam masalas, BIR mix powders and other Indian spice blends.
cuisine/indian/stocks: Spiced stocks used in base gravies and BIR curries.
cuisine/indonesian: Bold, layered cooking from across the Indonesian archipelago. Centred on chilli, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaf and shrimp paste, often softened with coconut milk and palm sugar. Long-braised rendang, sambal-led plates, satay grilling and quick-fried rice and noodle dishes show the range, where sweet, salty, spicy and sour all sit on the same plate.
cuisine/irish: Hearty, weather-driven cooking built on potatoes, root vegetables, lamb, beef and dairy. Flavours stay restrained, leaning on onion, parsley, mustard and the occasional pour of stout, letting good ingredients lead. One-pot stews, soda breads and griddled cakes reflect a tradition of slow, frugal home cooking.
cuisine/japanese: Precise, restrained cooking that prizes seasonality, simple knife work and the umami of dashi, miso and soy. Flavours come from soy sauce, mirin, sake, miso, kombu, bonito and the bright counterpoints of yuzu, ginger and pickled vegetables. Defining techniques include the long, slow extraction of dashi, careful poaching and simmering, the high-heat sear of yakimono, and the layered seasoning of a proper ramen broth and tare.
cuisine/italian: A tale of regions, where the same dish changes character every fifty miles: northern butter and rice, central pasta and pork, southern tomatoes, olives and seafood. Herbs (basil, rosemary, sage) and aged cheeses balance simple sauces against long-cooked ragùs. Hand-rolled pasta, slow braises, wood-fired baking and the patient build of risotto define the techniques.
cuisine/jamaican: Spicy, smoky cooking, fusing African, Indian and British colonial influences with native ingredients. Allspice, scotch bonnet, thyme, ginger and lime drive jerk marinades; coconut and beans anchor rice and one-pot stews. Slow grilling over pimento wood, escovitch frying and patient simmering of curries and stews carry the flavour.
cuisine/malaysian: A multicultural cuisine where Malay, Chinese (especially Hokkien), Tamil Indian and Peranakan (Nyonya) traditions sit on the same table, shaped by hawker culture and street food. Flavours come from coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaf, belacan (shrimp paste), pandan, tamarind and the sweet darkness of kecap manis. Defining techniques include the freshly pounded rempah spice paste, fast wok stir-fries for noodle dishes, slow-simmered coconut curries and the layered assembly of nasi lemak and laksa.
cuisine/mexican: A cuisine built on corn, beans and chillies (fresh, fermented and smoke-dried). Ancho, chipotle, lime, coriander and cumin work alongside tomatillo, avocado and crema. Comal-toasting of spices, slow-stewed adobos, charred salsas and griddled tortillas shape the table.
cuisine/pizza: Neapolitan and home-style pizzas, with high-hydration doughs slow-fermented for flavour and structure. Toppings stay restrained: san marzano tomato, fior di latte or fresh mozzarella, basil, olive oil and a few well-chosen extras. Whether wood-fired or baked at home, the goal is a charred, airy crust under fresh, simple toppings.
cuisine/spanish: Mediterranean cooking grounded in olive oil, garlic, smoked paprika and saffron. Cured pork (jamón, chorizo), seafood and short-grain rice anchor the table, in paella, fideuà, tortilla and the tapas tradition. Slow simmers, plancha-griddling and one-pot rice dishes dominate, with the sofrito (tomato, onion, garlic, paprika) starting nearly everything.
cuisine/thai: Cooking that balances four flavours in every dish: sweet, sour, salty and spicy. Built on lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaf, fish sauce, palm sugar and chilli, often rounded with coconut milk. Pounded curry pastes, fast wok work, raw salads dressed with lime and fish sauce, and aromatic broths like tom yum show the spread.
cuisine/thai/curry: Red, green, yellow, panang and massaman curries.
cuisine/thai/pastes: Curry pastes that anchor each Thai curry style.
cuisine/thai/salads: Som tam, glass noodle salads and other punchy Thai salads.
cuisine/thai/soup: Tom yum, tom kha and other Thai soups.
cuisine/thai/starters: Spring rolls, fishcakes, satay and other Thai openers.
cuisine/thai/stir-fries: Wok-fired meat, seafood and vegetable plates.
cuisine/vietnamese: Light, herb-driven cooking shaped by Chinese and French influences. Fish sauce, lime, mint, coriander, Thai basil and chilli give every dish its lift; rice noodles and rice paper supply the structure. Long-simmered broths like pho, grilled and fresh-rolled meats, and table-side assembly with herbs and lettuce define the experience.
desert: Sweet finishes: cakes, mousses, ice creams, soufflés and patisserie classics.
desert/chocolate: Chocolate-led desserts, including ganaches, tortes and roulades.
desert/creamy: Panna cotta, tiramisu and other rich, set creams.
desert/creme: Crème brûlée, custards and other set or chilled cream desserts.
desert/crepes: Sweet thin pancakes, folded, rolled and stuffed.
desert/french: Classic French patisserie, including éclairs, tarts and gateaux.
desert/mousse: Light, aerated chocolate and fruit mousses.
desert/pasta: Sweet pasta desserts. Uncommon, but worth the search.
desert/pastries: Hand-held sweets such as danishes, choux and the like.
petit-four: Bite-size sweets traditionally served with coffee at the end of a meal.
pies: Sweet and savoury pies, from rich meat fillings to buttery fruit bakes.
pies/meat-pies: Savoury pies, single and double crust.
pies/sweet-pies: Fruit-filled pies and traditional sweet bakes.
rice: Pilafs, biryanis, risottos and other rice-led dishes.
salad: Composed and tossed salads, from simple sides through to plated mains.
salsa: Fresh, raw and cooked salsas: chunky, herby, heat-graded.
sauces: A library of sauces (fish, meat, savoury, sweet, spicy and fruit-based) to pair with everything else here.
sauces/fruit-sauce: Sweet and tart fruit sauces for finishing plates.
sauces/sauce-fish: Sauces specifically for fish, including beurre blancs, hollandaise variations and more.
sauces/sauce-meat: Reductions, jus and pan sauces for red and white meat.
sauces/sauce-sweet: Sweet sauces for desserts: caramels, fruit reductions, chocolate.
sides: Vegetable preparations and starches that round out a plate.
soup: From clear broths to rich, blended soups, across Eastern and Western traditions.
sponge: Génoise, biscuit and other classic sponge cakes that anchor layered desserts.
starter: More substantial than appetisers: composed first courses to begin a meal.
stocks: The fundamentals: chicken, fish, brown and vegetable stocks that flavour soups and sauces.
tarts: Sweet and savoury tarts in shortcrust and puff pastry shells.
tarts/savoury: Quiches, vegetable tarts and other non-sweet pastries.
vinaigrette: Emulsified and broken vinaigrettes, classic and contemporary, for salads and crudités.
